narrative:
This morning at Tilden Regional Park, a chorus of American Crow calls echoed through the forest as they congregated in small groups, their distinctive caws mingling with the soft whistles of California Towhee. The air was crisp and clear, punctuated by the occasional flutter of wings from foraging birds, while the overcast sky provided just enough shade to keep temperatures comfortable at 14°C.

The quiet from Dark-eyed Junco and Oak Titmouse is not surprising: rain and wind push the acoustic noise floor high enough to bury softer songs, while the juncos have almost certainly departed for higher-elevation breeding grounds — their late-March peak here marked the end of winter residency, not a population. The towhee's dawn activity this time of year is territorial — breeding season opened earlier this month and pairs are patrolling their boundaries. The juncos are not coming back until autumn. Clear mornings ahead should bring Orange-crowned Warbler back into the record.
